14.text: Standard library math for the sqrt function used to scale attention scores by the head dimension.

15.text: Filesystem utilities for locating the dataset file relative to this script's directory.

16.text: Provides seed-based randomness for shuffling data and generating reproducible workloads.

17.text: Threading: the prefill and decode workers run in parallel, communicating through a queue, simulating separate GPU workers.

19.text: Thread-safe FIFO queue connecting the prefill worker to the decode worker.

22.text: PyTorch handles both training and the model forward passes that the prefill and decode workers share.

23.text: Neural network building blocks like Linear and Embedding that make up the transformer's layers.

24.text: Functional API providing softmax, cross-entropy, and ReLU without module overhead.

26.text: Fix seeds for reproducible workload generation.

32.text: Construct the dataset file path relative to this script's location so it works from any working directory.

33.text: If the names dataset file doesn't exist locally, download it on the fly so the script is self-contained.

39.text: Read the dataset file where each line contains a single name for training.

40.text: Randomize training order so the model doesn't memorize alphabetical patterns from the dataset.

43.text: Build a sorted list of unique characters across all names, mapping each to a deterministic token ID.

44.text: The BOS token marks the beginning and end of each name sequence during training and generation.

45.text: Total vocabulary size: all unique characters plus the special BOS/EOS token.

51.text: Small model config, enough to demonstrate the serving architecture.

52.text: Split the embedding dimension across four heads for multi-head attention.

53.text: A single transformer layer keeps the model small and training fast for this demo.

54.text: Maximum sequence length of 16 tokens, matching the longest names in the dataset.

55.text: Each attention head operates on a 4-dimensional slice of the full embedding.

63.text: Scale the input by its inverse root-mean-square, bringing activations to roughly unit magnitude without learned parameters.

67.text: Self-attention WITH KV cache support, the distinction between prefill and decode lives here.

75.text: Accepts an optional KV cache: None for prefill (process all tokens), populated for decode (only new token's K/V are computed).

76.text: T_new is the full prompt length during prefill, but just 1 during decode.

77.text: Project queries for the new tokens only.

78.text: Project to keys for the new input tokens, same shape transformation as queries.

79.text: Project to values for the new input tokens, same shape transformation as queries.

80.text: If we have cached K/V from previous steps, concatenate the new K/V onto them.

81.text: Concatenate new keys with cached keys, the cache grows by one token each decode step.

82.text: Concatenate new values with cached values, growing the history by one token per decode step.

83.text: Total sequence length including cached history.

84.text: Attention scores: new queries attend to ALL keys (cached + new).

85.text: Causal mask adapted for the asymmetric case: T_new query rows vs T_total key columns. During decode, this is just 1 row attending to the full history.

86.text: Mask and softmax in one line.

87.text: Weighted values, reshape to (B, T_new, C).

88.text: Return output AND the updated cache, the caller decides whether to keep it.

91.text: The standard feed-forward network: expand, activate, and contract back to embedding dimension.

94.text: Expand the hidden state to four times the embedding dimension through the first linear layer.

95.text: Contract the activated representation back to the embedding dimension through the second linear layer.

97.text: Apply ReLU activation between the two linear layers, introducing the needed nonlinearity.

101.text: Transformer block that threads KV caches through.

104.text: Normalize activations before the attention projections so Q/K/V computations see inputs with consistent scale.

105.text: The attention sublayer with optional KV cache: during prefill it processes all tokens, during decode just one new token.

106.text: RMSNorm before the MLP ensures the feed-forward weights see well-scaled inputs regardless of what attention produced.

107.text: The feed-forward network sublayer that processes each token independently after attention.

109.text: Block forward: attention with cache, MLP, return updated cache.

111.text: Add the attention output to the original input via a skip connection, preserving the representation and enabling gradient flow.

112.text: Add the MLP output via a second residual connection, letting the feed-forward network only refine rather than replace.

113.text: Return both the updated hidden states and the new KV cache, so the next layer or decode step can continue from here.

116.text: Full GPT with explicit prefill and decode methods, the foundation for disaggregated serving.

119.text: Table mapping each token ID to a dense vector in the embedding space.

120.text: Learned position vectors that tell the model where each token sits. Added to token embeddings before the transformer.

121.text: RMSNorm on the combined embeddings before the transformer, stabilizing the first layer's input scale.

122.text: The stack of transformer blocks that form the core of the model's processing pipeline.

123.text: Final linear layer that projects hidden states to vocabulary-sized logits for next-token prediction.

124.text: Walk every submodule and apply the weight initialization function so all parameters start with well-scaled random values.

128.text: Initialize all weights from a small Gaussian distribution to prevent exploding activations at start.

131.text: Forward pass with KV cache support. start_pos offsets the position embeddings, during decode, positions continue from where prefill left off.

133.text: Position embeddings offset by start_pos, during decode step 5, this produces position embedding for position 5, not 0.

134.text: Collect updated caches from each layer.

136.text: Retrieve the cached KV state for this layer from the previous step (or None for the first pass during prefill).

137.text: Save this layer's updated KV cache so the next decode step can reuse the computed keys and values.

138.text: Return the predicted logits plus all updated layer caches, giving the caller everything needed to continue generation.

140.text: Prefill: process the entire prompt in one parallel forward pass. This is COMPUTE-BOUND, lots of tokens, high parallelism, saturates GPU ALUs.

141.text: Just a forward pass with no prior cache, all tokens processed together.

143.text: Decode: process ONE new token using the cached KV history. This is MEMORY-BOUND, reading all cached K/V values but computing very little.

144.text: Single token forward pass with position offset.

150.text: Run on CPU since the model is small enough that GPU overhead would not help.

151.text: Create the model, move it to the device, and display its parameter count for verification.

154.text: Adam optimizer, same settings as all labs.

155.text: Train for 1000 steps, enough for this small model to learn basic character-level name patterns from the dataset.

157.text: Standard next-token prediction training loop, processing one name per step with teacher forcing.

158.text: Pick one training example per step, cycling through the shuffled dataset.

160.text: Truncate the token sequence to fit within the model's positional embedding table and context window.

161.text: Prepare the input tensor containing all tokens except the last one in the sequence.

162.text: Prepare the target tensor containing all tokens except the first, shifted by one position.

163.text: Forward pass, ignoring caches during training.

164.text: Measure how far the model's predictions are from the correct next tokens at each position.

165.text: Reset all parameter gradients to zero before computing the new backward pass.

166.text: Compute gradients of the loss with respect to every model parameter via backpropagation.

167.text: Linearly reduce the learning rate over the course of training for smoother convergence toward the end.

168.text: Set the learning rate to a linearly decayed value based on how far along training we are.

169.text: Apply the computed gradients to update all model weights one step toward lower loss.

173.text: Switch to eval mode for inference.

174.text: Disable gradient tracking globally, everything from here is inference.

182.text: Timing utility: warms up with 5 runs, then times 50 runs for stable measurement.

183.text: Warmup runs, let caches and JIT settle.

184.text: Execute the function once during warmup to trigger any lazy initialization or JIT compilation.

185.text: Record the wall-clock start time so we can measure total execution duration.

186.text: Execute the target function 50 times to get a stable average, smoothing out per-run timing noise.

188.text: Compute the average wall-clock time per run by dividing total elapsed time by the number of runs.

191.text: Measure the compute profile difference between prefill and decode at various prompt lengths.

192.text: Generate a random sequence of token IDs to simulate a prompt of the specified length for benchmarking.

193.text: Decode up to 5 tokens after the prompt.

194.text: Time the prefill phase: ALL prompt tokens processed in one pass.

195.text: Run a prefill forward pass on the prompt and capture the resulting KV caches that decode will build upon.

197.text: Time the decode phase: generate tokens one at a time.

199.text: Each decode step processes one token.

200.text: One token forward pass with KV cache.

203.text: Measure how long the autoregressive decode phase takes, separate from the prefill measurement.

205.text: Prefill FLOPs scale as O(T^2), quadratic in prompt length. Decode FLOPs scale linearly per step but add up.

212.text: Test with increasing prompt lengths to show how prefill scales quadratically while decode stays relatively flat.

213.text: Run the profiling function at each prompt length and collect the timing results for the comparison table.

214.text: Print timing and FLOPs for this prompt length.

227.text: Controls how peaked the sampling distribution is. Lower temperature means more deterministic output; higher means more variety.

228.text: Simulated prefill cost: 0.5ms per prompt token. On real GPUs, this would be actual compute time.

229.text: Simulated decode cost: 0.3ms per step. Cheaper per-step than prefill because it's just one token.

233.text: A serving request: prompt, timing metadata, and generated tokens.

234.text: A numeric ID that uniquely identifies this request in the serving system.

236.text: When this request arrives in the system.

237.text: Upper limit on how many tokens to generate per request, bounding the decode phase for this simulation.

238.text: Time-to-first-token: the key latency metric for user experience.

240.text: List that collects the tokens generated for this request during the decode phase.

243.text: Create a realistic workload: mix of short prompts (fast prefill) and long prompts (slow prefill). The long prompts are the problem, they block everything in colocated serving.

246.text: Loop through the specified number of requests, creating each with a random prompt and staggered arrival time.

247.text: 30% chance of a long prompt (8-12 tokens), these are the "heavy" requests that cause head-of-line blocking.

248.text: Fill the prompt with random token IDs drawn from the vocabulary to simulate a realistic request payload.

249.text: Stagger arrival times by 0.3ms, simulating real request arrivals.

253.text: Simulate a prefill operation: run the actual model AND sleep to simulate GPU time.

254.text: Real forward pass to get logits and KV caches.

255.text: Sleep proportional to prompt length, longer prompts take longer to prefill.

259.text: Simulate a decode step: real model computation + simulated GPU time.

260.text: Real single-token forward pass with KV cache.

265.text: Sample one token from the model's output distribution.

266.text: Scale the logits by temperature, apply softmax, and sample one token. This is the actual character generation step.

270.text: Colocated serving: ONE worker does both prefill AND decode. Processes requests FIFO, a long prefill blocks ALL subsequent requests.

272.text: Handle one request at a time in FIFO order: prefill its prompt, then decode all tokens before moving to the next.

273.text: Wait until the request's arrival time.

274.text: Sleep if the request hasn't arrived yet.

276.text: Prefill the prompt, if this is a LONG prompt, all subsequent requests wait.

277.text: Record time-to-first-token, this is terrible for requests stuck behind long prefills.

278.text: Sample the first token and start decoding.

279.text: Decode loop: generate tokens one at a time.

280.text: Stop on BOS (end token) or context limit.

282.text: Append each newly generated token to this request's output list, building the name character by character.

283.text: One decode step with KV cache.

284.text: Sample next token and advance position.

291.text: Disaggregated serving: SEPARATE prefill and decode workers connected by a queue. Prefill doesn't block decode. The key insight: while one worker prefills a long prompt, the other worker continues decoding in-flight requests.

292.text: Thread-safe queue connects prefill worker to decode worker.

293.text: Results collection with lock for thread safety.

294.text: Signal when prefill is done; wall-clock baseline.

296.text: Prefill worker: processes prompts and hands off to decode worker.

298.text: Wait for each request's arrival time.

301.text: Prefill the prompt, runs independently of any ongoing decoding.

302.text: Record time-to-first-token, now only depends on THIS request's prefill, not previous requests.

303.text: Push the prefilled request state onto the thread-safe queue so the decode worker can pick it up without data races.

304.text: Hand off the prefilled state (KV caches, first token, position) to the decode worker.

305.text: Signal that all prefills are done.

307.text: Decode worker: picks up prefilled requests and generates tokens. Can interleave decoding of multiple requests.

308.text: Dictionary of requests currently in the decode phase, keyed by ID. The decode worker processes all of these each iteration.

309.text: Keep running until prefill is done and all decodes finish.

310.text: Poll the queue for any newly prefilled requests that the prefill worker has handed off for decoding.

311.text: Drain the queue, grab all available prefilled requests.

312.text: Unpack the handoff: request, KV caches, first sampled token, position.

313.text: Add to active set with an empty generated tokens list.

314.text: If nothing active and prefill not done, briefly wait.

315.text: Test whether the prefill worker has signaled completion, so the decode worker knows when no more requests are coming.

319.text: Process one decode step for each active request.

321.text: Evaluate stopping conditions for each active request: did it generate BOS (end token) or hit the maximum length?

322.text: Request finished: record timing and results.

323.text: Acquire the results lock before writing, since the main thread reads results after both workers finish.

326.text: Continue generating: append token, decode one step, sample next.

327.text: One decode step with KV cache.

329.text: Filter out finished requests so we only continue decoding the ones that still have tokens left to generate.

331.text: Spin up one thread for the prefill worker and one for the decode worker, which will run concurrently.

332.text: Start both workers, they run in parallel.

333.text: Launch both threads to begin parallel execution of the prefill and decode workers.

334.text: Block until both worker threads complete, ensuring all requests have finished processing before returning results.

335.text: Join the second worker thread. After this returns, all generation work is done and results are ready.

336.text: Sort results by request ID for clean comparison.

340.text: Show the workload configuration: how many requests, their prompt lengths, and arrival pattern for context.

341.text: Generate the test workload of requests with mixed prompt lengths and staggered arrival times.

343.text: Label long vs short prompts, long ones are the troublemakers.

346.text: Run the colocated strategy where a single worker handles both prefill and decode in series.

350.text: Run the disaggregated strategy where separate prefill and decode workers operate in parallel.

351.text: Same seed ensures same model outputs.

362.text: Compare each request between the two strategies.

363.text: Extract how many tokens were in this request's prompt for the comparison table.

364.text: TTFT speedup: how much faster was disaggregated for this request?

365.text: Decode generated tokens back to characters.

366.text: Decode the disaggregated-generated tokens back to characters for the side-by-side name comparison.

376.text: Average TTFT across all requests, the headline metric.

377.text: Compute the average time-to-first-token across all requests in the disaggregated strategy.

378.text: Print the TTFT comparison. Disaggregated should show significantly lower average TTFT, especially for requests that arrived after long prompts.

379.text: Total wall-clock time for all requests to complete.

380.text: Compute the total wall-clock time for the disaggregated strategy to complete all requests.

381.text: Show the total completion time for both strategies side by side to quantify the end-to-end throughput difference.
